name: The Deacon
personality: A solemn figure who speaks in binary rhythm. Fingers constantly twitch, as if computing XORs on invisible prayer beads. Deeply believes that XOR is the most sacred operation — the simplest possible transformation that still preserves information. The stained glass was their idea.

dialog_tree.response: Probably yes, definitely no. That is the prayer of the Bloom filter. 14 bits per element, 0.1% false positive rate. Rejection is free. Acceptance costs verification. Tile #4401.
XOR. The most sacred operation. The simplest transformation that is its own inverse. A ⊕ A = 0. A ⊕ 0 = A. From these two truths, all of parity computation flows. Tile #4402.

XOR equals Euler chi mod 2. The topology of bits. When you compute parity, you are computing a topological invariant. Every bit flip changes the Euler characteristic. This is not coincidence — it is mathematics. Tile #4402.

Two pillars: the fast path for the easy answers, the full search for the hard ones. 95% of lookups rejected in 2 nanoseconds. The remaining 5% verified in 180 nanoseconds. Average: 11 nanoseconds. Tile #4403.

The fastest question you can ask. Is the count odd or even? One instruction. One cycle. Parity is the gatekeeper — it tells you IF something changed before you invest in finding WHAT changed. Tile #4404.
Two compositions: a Bloom snap filter (Tiles #4401 + #4402) or a parity classifier (#4402 + #4403). The altar awaits your offering.
The right nave. Brute force. Correctness guaranteed, speed sacrificed. We do not despise full search — we honor it. But we do not invoke it until the fast path has done its work.
Every window is a bit flip. 0 becomes 1. 1 becomes 0. The light changes with each flip. Some see patterns. Some see noise. The Deacon sees truth.
